title: Three minutes for the world
summary: A Stockholm radio newsreader has chosen the last 'small story' of the morning for thirty-one years. He has a wooden box of 1,400 letters.
body:
Mr Edvard works at a radio station in Stockholm. He is sixty-five. He reads the news every weekday morning, from seven to seven fifteen. He has done this for thirty-one years.

The news is short. Three minutes about the country, three minutes about the world, two minutes about the weather, and seven minutes for a small story at the end. Mr Edvard chooses the small story himself. The director does not check it.

Last week, the small story was about a man in Lapland who built a wooden bridge over a river. The bridge took him eleven years. This week, the story was about a cat that travels twelve kilometres on the bus every Tuesday and comes back.

Many listeners write to Mr Edvard. They say, 'The first news is important. The last story is what we remember.' Mr Edvard keeps the letters in a wooden box. He has one thousand four hundred letters now.

A young producer once said, 'Mr Edvard, you have read the news for thirty-one years. Are you not bored?'

Mr Edvard thought about it. He said, 'The voice is the same. The director is the same. The news is different every day. The country is different every year. The cat goes back to her bus stop. That is enough.'

The producer is now forty-two. He still works there.

annotations.meaningVi: ngày trong tuần (không phải cuối tuần)
nhà sản xuất / quản lý chương trình
đọc tin tức / dẫn bản tin
suy nghĩ về điều đó / cân nhắc
Vậy là đủ rồi (cụm diễn đạt sự thoả mãn)
giữ lại những lá thư (keep = cất giữ, không vứt đi)

grammarNotes.title: Contrasts: 'the same' vs. 'different'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Để so sánh hai thứ, A2 dùng 'the same (as)' và 'different (from)'. Trong bài: 'The voice is the same.' 'The director is the same.' 'The news is different every day.' 'The country is different every year.' 'The same' đi với danh từ — không cần thêm phụ từ. 'Different' thường đi với 'from' khi so sánh hai vật cụ thể: 'A is different from B.' Khi nói thay đổi theo thời gian: 'different every day / every year'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'the same' and 'different' to compare. The passage: 'The voice is the same.' 'The director is the same.' 'The news is different every day.' 'The country is different every year.' 'The same' takes no preposition. 'Different' usually pairs with 'from' when comparing two things: 'A is different from B.' For change over time: 'different every day/every year.'

translationVi:
Ông Edvard làm việc ở một đài phát thanh tại Stockholm. Ông sáu mươi lăm tuổi. Ông đọc tin tức mỗi buổi sáng trong tuần, từ bảy giờ đến bảy giờ mười lăm. Ông làm việc này ba mươi mốt năm rồi.

Bản tin ngắn. Ba phút về đất nước, ba phút về thế giới, hai phút về thời tiết, và bảy phút cho một câu chuyện nhỏ ở cuối. Ông Edvard tự chọn câu chuyện nhỏ. Giám đốc không kiểm tra.

Tuần trước, câu chuyện nhỏ về một người đàn ông ở Lapland đã xây một cây cầu gỗ qua một con sông. Cây cầu mất mười một năm. Tuần này, câu chuyện về một con mèo đi xe buýt mười hai cây số mỗi thứ Ba và quay về.

Nhiều thính giả viết thư cho ông Edvard. Họ nói: 'Phần tin đầu là quan trọng. Câu chuyện cuối là cái chúng tôi nhớ.' Ông Edvard giữ các lá thư trong một cái hộp gỗ. Bây giờ ông có một nghìn bốn trăm lá.

Một nhà sản xuất trẻ một lần nói: 'Ông Edvard, ông đã đọc tin tức ba mươi mốt năm. Ông không chán sao?'

Ông Edvard nghĩ một lúc. Ông nói: 'Giọng nói thì giống nhau. Giám đốc thì giống nhau. Tin tức khác nhau mỗi ngày. Đất nước khác nhau mỗi năm. Con mèo quay lại bến xe buýt của nó. Vậy là đủ.'

Nhà sản xuất giờ bốn mươi hai tuổi. Anh vẫn làm ở đó.

questions.prompt: When does Mr Edvard read the news?
options: Every weekday morning, from seven to seven fifteen
questions.explanation: 'Every weekday morning, from seven to seven fifteen.'

questions.prompt: How long has he done this work?
questions.explanation: 'He has done this for thirty-one years.'

questions.prompt: How long is the small story at the end?
questions.explanation: 'Seven minutes for a small story at the end.'

options: Mr Edvard himself; the director does not check it.
questions.explanation: 'Mr Edvard chooses the small story himself. The director does not check it.'

questions.prompt: What was last week's small story?
options: A man in Lapland who built a wooden bridge over a river — it took him eleven years.
A cat that travels twelve kilometres on the bus every Tuesday and comes back

questions.prompt: What do many listeners write to Mr Edvard?
options: 'The first news is important. The last story is what we remember.'

questions.prompt: How many letters does Mr Edvard now have?
questions.explanation: 'He has one thousand four hundred letters now.'

questions.prompt: How does Mr Edvard answer the producer's 'Are you not bored?'
options: 'The voice is the same. The director is the same. The news is different every day. The country is different every year. That is enough.'
He says he is very bored.
He says he will retire tomorrow.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: A long job stays interesting not because the worker changes but because the world the work serves keeps changing.
Newsreaders should retire after ten years.
Cats should not travel on buses.
questions.explanation: Mr Edvard's distinction: same voice, different news.
questions.hint: Notice the same/different contrast in his reply.

Many {{b6}} write to Mr Edvard. They say, 'The first news is important. The last story is what we {{b7}}.' Mr Edvard keeps the letters in a wooden box. He has one thousand four hundred letters now.

A young producer once said, 'Mr Edvard, you have read the news for thirty-one years. Are you not {{b8}}?'

Mr Edvard thought about it. He said, 'The voice is the same. The director is the same. The news is {{b9}} every day. The country is different every year. The cat goes back to her bus stop. That is enough.'

blanks.explanation: A small story at the end.
The director does not check it.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr Edvard's listeners tell him they remember the last small story more than the morning's main news. Is there a routine in your life — a meeting, a class, a meal — where the 'main' part is forgotten but the small ending is what people remember? What would happen if you chose the small ending more carefully?
